us-bibliographic-data-grant.invention-title: System and Method for Semantic Document Parsing

abstract.p: A system and method for parsing documents into semantic RDF knowledge graphs. The system includes a plurality of format-specific backend parsers, an embedded RDF datastore, and a SPARQL query interface. Documents are converted to RDF triples representing structural and semantic relationships, enabling advanced querying and analysis.

description.heading: FIELD OF THE INVENTION
description.p: This invention relates generally to document processing systems, and more particularly to systems and methods for parsing documents into semantic knowledge graphs using Resource Description Framework (RDF) standards.
description.heading: BACKGROUND
description.p: Traditional document parsing systems convert documents to proprietary data structures that limit interoperability and semantic querying capabilities. There is a need for a standards-based approach that represents documents as queryable knowledge graphs.
description.heading: SUMMARY
description.p: The present invention provides a document parsing system that converts documents from various formats (PDF, DOCX, HTML, Markdown, LaTeX, etc.) into RDF knowledge graphs stored in an embedded Oxigraph datastore.
The system comprises format-specific backend parsers that map document structures to a common ontology, an RDF triple store for persistence, and SPARQL query capabilities for semantic search.
description.heading: DETAILED DESCRIPTION
description.p: Referring now to the drawings, the document parsing system 100 comprises a document converter 110, a plurality of backend parsers 120, an RDF graph store 130, and an export pipeline 140.

The document converter 110 receives an input document in any supported format and selects an appropriate backend parser 120 based on format detection. The backend parser 120 analyzes the document structure and generates RDF triples conforming to the document ontology.

The RDF graph store 130 is preferably implemented using Oxigraph, an embedded SPARQL database written in Rust. Each parsed document is stored in a separate named graph identified by a content-addressable hash.

The document ontology defines classes including Document, Page, TextElement, SectionHeader, Paragraph, TableElement, TableCell, PictureElement, and others. Properties define relationships such as hasElement, textContent, readingOrder, headingLevel, and parentElement.

The export pipeline 140 supports multiple output formats including Markdown, HTML, JSON, WebVTT, Turtle, N-Triples, JSON-LD, and RDF/XML. Exporters query the document graph via SPARQL and reconstruct the document in the target format.

claim.claim-text: 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the document ontology defines classes comprising Document, Page, TextElement, SectionHeader, Paragraph, ListItem, TableElement, TableCell, PictureElement, Code, and Formula.

3. The method of claim 1, wherein the document ontology defines properties comprising textContent, readingOrder, headingLevel, parentElement, childElement, nextElement, cellRow, cellColumn, cellRowSpan, and cellColSpan.

4. The method of claim 1, further comprising: executing a SPARQL query against the named graph to retrieve document elements matching specified criteria; and exporting the retrieved elements in an output format selected from the group consisting of Markdown, HTML, JSON, Turtle, N-Triples, JSON-LD, and RDF/XML.
5. The method of claim 1, wherein the RDF datastore is implemented using Oxigraph.
7. The system of claim 6, wherein the plurality of backend parsers includes parsers for PDF, DOCX, HTML, Markdown, LaTeX, CSV, XLSX, PPTX, XML, WebVTT, and AsciiDoc formats.
